quiz.title: Unit 2 Quiz 3, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Subatomic Particles: Protons, Neutrons, and Electrons
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: Properties of Subatomic Particles: Charge, Mass, Location; Atomic Number, Mass Number; Neutral Atoms; Isotopes; History of Particle Discovery

questions.question: Which subatomic particle has a negative charge?
questions.explanation: Electrons have a negative charge (-1). Protons are positive (+1), and neutrons have no charge (0). Positrons are positive but are antimatter particles, not typically found in ordinary atoms.

questions.question: Which subatomic particle has no charge?
questions.explanation: Neutrons are neutral particles with no electrical charge. They are found in the nucleus along with positively charged protons.

questions.question: Which particle is located in the nucleus of an atom?
questions.explanation: Protons and neutrons are located in the nucleus. Electrons orbit the nucleus in electron clouds or orbitals.

questions.question: Which subatomic particle has a mass approximately equal to 1 amu?
questions.explanation: Both protons and neutrons have masses of approximately 1 atomic mass unit (amu). Electrons are much lighter (about 1/1836 amu).

questions.question: Which particle is least massive?
questions.explanation: Electrons are the least massive subatomic particle, with a mass of about 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg (approximately 1/1836 of a proton's mass).

questions.question: Which combination correctly gives the charge of an atom if it's neutral?
questions.explanation: A neutral atom has equal numbers of protons (positive charge) and electrons (negative charge), balancing the overall charge to zero.

questions.question: What is the mass number of an atom?
questions.explanation: Mass number (A) is the total number of protons and neutrons in the nucleus. Atomic number (Z) is the number of protons only.

questions.question: Which subatomic particle determines the chemical identity of an element?
questions.explanation: The number of protons (atomic number) determines the element. Changing protons changes the element; changing neutrons creates isotopes; changing electrons creates ions.

questions.question: Which particle contributes to isotopes of an element?
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same number of protons) with different numbers of neutrons, resulting in different mass numbers.

questions.question: A neutral atom has 8 protons. How many electrons does it have?
questions.explanation: In a neutral atom, the number of electrons equals the number of protons. With 8 protons, it must have 8 electrons (this is oxygen).

questions.question: Which particle is responsible for chemical bonding?
questions.explanation: Electrons, specifically valence electrons in the outermost shells, are responsible for chemical bonding through sharing, gaining, or losing electrons.

questions.question: Which particle was discovered first?
questions.explanation: The electron was discovered first by J.J. Thomson in 1897. The proton was discovered by Rutherford in 1919, and the neutron by Chadwick in 1932.

questions.question: Which subatomic particle contributes almost all the mass of an atom?
questions.explanation: Protons and neutrons (nucleons) are located in the nucleus and contain nearly all the atom's mass. Electrons contribute very little mass.

questions.question: Which of the following is a positively charged particle in the nucleus?
questions.explanation: Protons are positively charged particles found in the nucleus. Neutrons are neutral, and electrons (negative) are outside the nucleus.

questions.question: Which particle was discovered by J.J. Thomson?
questions.explanation: J.J. Thomson discovered the electron in 1897 through his cathode ray tube experiments, proving that atoms were divisible.

scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review the properties of protons, neutrons, and electrons.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on charge, mass, and location of each particle.
scoreMessages.0: Please review the basic properties of subatomic particles and their roles in atoms.
